comment_text.sn3.2:1.3: While we cannot trace Pasenadi’s progress with confidence, we can see that here he has gone from questioning the Buddha’s awakening to learning fundamentals of Buddhist psychology.
comment_text.sn3.2:3.4: Tacasāra (Sanskrit tvaksāra) is “that which has hardwood for its skin”, namely the bamboo (see SN 6.12:2.2).

root_text.sn3.2:1.2: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho rājā pasenadi kosalo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn3.2:1.3: “kati nu kho, bhante, purisassa dhammā ajjhattaṁ uppajjamānā uppajjanti ahitāya dukkhāya aphāsuvihārāyā”ti?
root_text.sn3.2:2.1: “Tayo kho, mahārāja, purisassa dhammā ajjhattaṁ uppajjamānā uppajjanti ahitāya dukkhāya aphāsuvihārāya.
root_text.sn3.2:2.3: Lobho kho, mahārāja, purisassa dhammo ajjhattaṁ uppajjamāno uppajjati ahitāya dukkhāya aphāsuvihārāya.
root_text.sn3.2:2.4: Doso kho, mahārāja, purisassa dhammo ajjhattaṁ uppajjamāno uppajjati ahitāya dukkhāya aphāsuvihārāya.
root_text.sn3.2:2.5: Moho kho, mahārāja, purisassa dhammo ajjhattaṁ uppajjamāno uppajjati ahitāya dukkhāya aphāsuvihārāya.
root_text.sn3.2:2.6: Ime kho, mahārāja, tayo purisassa dhammā ajjhattaṁ uppajjamānā uppajjanti ahitāya dukkhāya aphāsuvihārāyā”ti.

translation_text.sn3.2:1.2: Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn3.2:1.3: “Sir, how many things arise inside a person for their harm, suffering, and discomfort?”
translation_text.sn3.2:2.1: “Great king, three things arise inside a person for their harm, suffering, and discomfort.
translation_text.sn3.2:2.6: These three things arise inside a person for their harm, suffering, and discomfort.”
translation_text.sn3.2:2.7: That is what the Buddha said. …
translation_text.sn3.2:3.3: they harm a person of wicked heart,
translation_text.sn3.2:3.4: as a bamboo is destroyed by its own fruit.”
